title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Impact of Fast Fashion on Global Culture

texts.A: The rise of ultra-fast fashion is often defended as the democratization of style, but this is a shallow argument that ignores the systematic erosion of local textile traditions. When global conglomerates can replicate a traditional design and mass-produce it in synthetic fabrics within days, the original cultural significance is stripped away. Consumers are being trained to value novelty over longevity, leading to a psychological disconnect from the labor and resources required to produce a single garment. We are not just creating landfill waste; we are impoverishing our global aesthetic diversity.
texts.B: To blame the fashion industry for environmental degradation is to ignore the power of the consumer. Fast fashion has provided millions of people with the ability to express their identity through clothing—a privilege once reserved for the wealthy. The speed of the supply chain is a marvel of modern logistics, not a crime. If there is a problem with sustainability, the solution lies in developing better recycling technologies, not in shaming individuals for wanting to look current. Progress is about making high-end aesthetics accessible to the masses.
texts.C: There is a middle ground between the 'buy nothing' extremists and the fast-fashion addicts. The real issue is the lack of transparency in the manufacturing process. I share the view that the sheer speed of production has led to a catastrophic decline in quality, which in turn fuels the throwaway culture. However, I disagree that this is a 'democratization' of style. True democratization would mean fair wages for the artisans at the start of the chain, not just cheap prices for the teenagers at the end. We need a 'slow fashion' movement that prioritizes the ethics of production over the speed of delivery.
texts.D: The environmental impact of the garment industry is undeniable, yet the focus on 'fast fashion' as the sole villain is misplaced. Luxury brands are often just as guilty of overproduction and resource depletion. The problem is a global economic system that demands infinite growth on a finite planet. While some argue that consumers are being 'trained' to be wasteful, I believe people are simply responding to the incentives provided by the market. Until the true cost of carbon and water is reflected in the price tag of every shirt—whether it costs five dollars or five hundred—nothing will change.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on whether fast fashion represents the 'democratization' of style?
questions.explanation: Expert B sees fast fashion as a privilege that democratizes style. Expert C explicitly disagrees, stating that true democratization would require fair wages for artisans, not just cheap prices.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar opinion to A regarding the psychological effect of fast fashion on consumers?
questions.explanation: Expert A mentions consumers are being 'trained to value novelty over longevity.' Expert D also notes that people are 'responding to the incentives' of the market to be wasteful, agreeing that behavior is being shaped by the industry.

questions.question: Which expert shares C's concern about the decline in the quality of garments in the modern era?
questions.explanation: Expert C notes a 'catastrophic decline in quality.' Expert A supports this by stating that mass-produced 'synthetic fabrics' replace original designs and that the focus is on 'novelty over longevity.'

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from D on who is primarily responsible for the fashion industry's problems?
questions.explanation: Expert D blames the 'global economic system.' Expert B, however, places the responsibility (and power) on the 'power of the consumer' and their choices.
